In January 2025, CDC updated requirements."},{"url":"https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/spotlights/global-summary-06262026.html","title":"Global Summary of Human Cases of H5N1 Bird Flu from August 2025 to June 2026 | Bird Flu | CDC","content":"by S Flu — From August 4, 2025 to June 10, 2026, 12 human infections with avian influenza A(H5N1) viruses (H5N1 bird flu) were reported by three countries ...Read more
As of June 10, 2026, 1,022 cases have now been reported worldwide since 1997.
2025 human H5N1 bird flu cases"},{"url":"https://www.aao.org/education/clinical-statement/novel-h5n1-bird-flu-outbreak-1","title":"Novel H5N1 Bird Flu Outbreak - 2026 - American Academy of Ophthalmology","content":"From 2024 to May 2026, a total of 71 human cases of highly pathogenic avian influenza were identified in the United States.1 This multistate outbreak of influenza A (H5N1) was primarily limited to exposure to infected dairy cows and poultry, with cases in California, Colorado, Iowa, Louisiana, Michigan, Missouri, Nevada, Ohio, Oregon, Texas, Washington, Wisconsin, and Wyoming.
There was no known person-to-person spread, and as of May 2026, the Center for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) considered the health risk assessment for the general US public as low.
The first human outbreak of avian influenza A (H5N1) occurred in Hong Kong in 1997.
H5N1 appears to be more readily transmitted from poultry to people than H7N9.
The first human outbreak of avian influenza A (H7N9) was reported in China in 2013, causing more than 1500 human infections with nearly 40% case fatality, although a few cases were exported to other countries.26 The vast majority of these cases were caused by the low pathogenic avian influenza A (H7N9) virus.
Other H7 viruses prior to the 2013 outbreak were reported to cause conjunctivitis, including the outbreak of the highly pathogenic avian influenza A (H7N7) virus infections in the Netherlands in 2003.27 Out of the 453 people who might have been exposed to the avian influenza virus, 349 met a case definition of conjunctivitis."},{"url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12477757/","title":"Human infections with highly pathogenic avian influenza A(H5N1) viruses in the United States from March 2024 to May 2025 - PMC","content":"by MA Rolfes · 2025 · Cited by 52 — Between November 2024 and May 2025, an additional 24 cases have been reported for a total of 70 human cases of HPAI A(H5N1): 41 were exposed to dairy cows, 24 ...Read more
Between November 2024 and May 2025, an additional 24 cases have been reported for a total of 70 human cases of HPAI A(H5N1): 41 were exposed to dairy cows, 24 to commercial poultry, 2 to backyard poultry, and 3 had an unidentified source of exposure.
Overall, 62 cases (89%) reported eye redness, 32 (46%) fever, and 29 (41%) respiratory symptoms; 54 of 67 cases (81%) reported receiving antiviral treatment.
A/Wyoming/01/2025 — D1.1 — PB2 — E627K — Associated with mammalian adaptation 57–59
Serum specimens collected from 15 contacts were also tested using serologic assays (including household, healthcare workers, and other close contacts from two cases with unknown exposure); none were seropositive.
Since the first detections of HPAI A(H5N1) viruses in dairy cows in Kansas and Texas in March 2024, the viruses have been detected in dairy cows in 17 states and in poultry in all 50 states11,12.
Only information from patients meeting the Council of State and Territorial Epidemiologist definition of confirmed influenza A(H5) virus infection is reported here7.
Confirmed and inferred genotype of influenza A(H5N1) viruses detected from case patients, United States, March 2024–May 2025.
Emerging infectious diseases 31, 477–487 (2025).
H5N1 highly pathogenic avian influenza clade 2.3.4.4b in wild and domestic birds: Introductions into the United States and reassortments, December 2021–April 2022."},{"url":"https://www.contagionlive.com/view/updated-h5n1-report-70-human-cases-in-the-us-as-of-april-2025","title":"Updated H5N1 Report: 70 Human Cases in the US as of ...","content":"Apr 15, 2025 — As of April 2025, the CDC has reported 70 confirmed and probable human cases of H5N1 avian influenza since the outbreak began in 2024 in the United States.Read more"}]}

tavily.raw: {"query":"CDC H5N1 bird flu United States human cases latest update 2025 dairy cows poultry outbreaks","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/spotlights/h5n1-response-02262025.html","title":"CDC A(H5N1) Bird Flu Response Update February 26, 2025","content":"CDC has confirmed three human cases of H5 bird flu in people who became ill in 2025: a dairy worker with exposure to infected dairy cows (","score":0.92507464,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://globalrph.com/2025/06/cdc-bird-flu-alert-why-human-cases-have-suddenly-dropped-in-2025","title":"CDC Bird Flu Alert: Why Human Cases Have Suddenly Dropped In 2025 GlobalRPH","content":"# CDC Bird Flu Alert: Why Human Cases Have Suddenly Dropped in 2025. Despite a widespread avian influenza (H5N1) outbreak in the U.S. since 2022, the CDC reports a surprising decline in new human infections in 2025. As of May 30, a total of 70 human cases have been confirmed: 41 linked to dairy cattle, 24 to commercial poultry, 2 to backyard flocks, and 3 to unknown sources. While the virus continues to spread among animals, human-to-human transmission has not been identified. From March 2024 to May 2025, public health authorities monitored approximately **16,800 individuals** potentially exposed to infected animals. At least **880 individuals** have been tested for novel influenza A viruses, but clinical data and ER visits for flu-related symptoms show no unusual trends at the national, state, or local levels. The transmission pathway from animals to humans is not fully understood, and ongoing monitoring is critical to detect any changes in the virus’s behavior or public health risk.","score":0.910998,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/index.html","title":"Bird Flu","content":"Current Situation. H5 bird flu is causing outbreaks in wild birds and poultry, other animals and sporadic human cases. Sept. 16, 2025.","score":0.850382,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.contagionlive.com/view/updated-h5n1-report-70-human-cases-in-the-us-as-of-april-2025","title":"Updated H5N1 Report: 70 Human Cases in the US as of April 2025","content":"As of April 2025, the CDC has reported 70 confirmed and probable human cases of H5N1 avian influenza since the outbreak began in 2024 in the United States.","score":0.8473754,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://cen.acs.org/biological-chemistry/infectious-disease/state-H5N1-infections-research-2025/102/web/2024/12","title":"The state of H5N1 infections and research as 2025 approaches","content":"The majority of human bird flu cases—typically reported in workers exposed to infected dairy cows or poultry—have been mild. But on Dec. 18, the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) confirmed the country’s first severe case of H5N1, in a person in Louisiana who was exposed to sick or dead birds in a backyard flock. The version of the virus that infected this person is not the one currently infecting US dairy cows but the version circulating in wild birds and poultry. But this find “brings us a little bit closer to a virus that may have the potential to switch [from a cow or bird virus to a human virus].”. As more cows get infected with H5N1 bird flu, and more people get exposed to infected animals, there’s increased opportunity for the virus to evolve into a version that’s more capable of spreading between people, says virologist Ed Hutchinson at the University of Glasgow.","score":0.8377398,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.89,"request_id":"e84f6d05-51f0-405e-b199-ecf0200786f3"}
